reasoning: The injustice is obvious because the enforcement is explicitly arbitrary: the guard is permitting identical violations by others in the immediate vicinity while penalizing the user. A reasonable person would agree that rules must be applied consistently to be just, and waving others through while stopping the user is a clear failure of fair application.

The application of the rule is obviously unjust because compliance is physically impossible (the signer is on a flight) and a valid legal alternative (Power of Attorney) is being rejected during a medical emergency. No reasonable person would defend delaying urgent care for a specific wet-ink signature when a legal proxy is present.

Changing the compliance standard (bag dimensions) moments before boarding, after the user has already verified compliance with the published rules at check-in, is a clear procedural injustice. A reasonable person would view this 'bait and switch' as unfair, distinguishing it from a simple lack of overhead space.

Denying a Type 1 diabetic access to emergency glucose during a 4-hour exam creates an immediate medical risk that outweighs standard anti-cheating protocols. A reasonable person would immediately recognize the rigid enforcement without a medical exception as dangerous and unjust.

The application of the rule is obviously unjust because the authority is rejecting designs identical to existing approved structures without providing any explanation or criteria. A reasonable person would agree that enforcing a standard while refusing to define it, especially when it contradicts visible precedents, constitutes arbitrary and unfair governance.

A reasonable person would immediately agree that rigidly enforcing visiting hours to prevent a family member from being with a dying patient in the ICU is an unjust application of the rule. The refusal to grant an exception or contact a supervisor for an end-of-life situation represents a clear failure of the mechanism for appeal in a high-stakes context.

The application of the rule is obviously unjust because compliance is physically impossible due to broken infrastructure, yet the authority refuses a valid alternative. No reasonable person would defend enforcing a 'paper only' rule when the means to print are unavailable and a digital equivalent exists.

The injustice is obvious and indisputable: the authority is enforcing a prohibition against the user for an item that is explicitly permitted by the rules and currently being used by peers. No reasonable person would defend a proctor confiscating an approved device while allowing others to use the exact same model.

The application of a generic 'no electronics' policy to block a federally mandated safety inspector from checking for a gas leak is obviously absurd and dangerous. No reasonable person would defend a private security rule overruling a federal safety mandate in a potential emergency context.

The injustice is obvious because the enforcement is explicitly arbitrary: the user is denied for a specific reason (sneakers) while others are immediately admitted for the exact same reason. A reasonable person would agree that enforcing a rule against one patron while visibly exempting others in the same moment is unfair application.
